zh.lifeSummary: 當Richard Cecil ap Philip SEISYLLT of Alt-yr-Ynys於1425年之後出生在威爾士蒙姆爾時，他的父親Sir Richard Cecil8629歲，母親Margaret Phillips8574歲。他於1468年與Margaret Maud VAUGHAN在英國英格蘭格盧斯特斯爾布利結婚。他們至少是6個兒子和1個女兒的父母。他去世於1508年在英國林可爾史爾。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Richard Cecil ap Philip SEISYLLT of Alt-yr-Ynys于1425年之后出生在威尔士蒙姆爾时，他的父亲Sir Richard Cecil8629岁，母亲Margaret Phillips8574岁。他于1468年与Margaret Maud VAUGHAN在英国英格兰格盧斯特斯爾布利结婚。他们至少是6个儿子和1个女儿的父母。他去世于1508年在英国林可爾史爾。

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese: Cheng, Jing, Kam Fung, Wai Shing, Wing, Yali, Yuk.
variant Romanization of the surnames 歐 and 區, see   Ou   1 and 2.

variants.name: Ou
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Wei, Ying, Ching, Heng, Hong, Jia, Kuang Yu, Li-Ching, Cho, Chong, Chung, Sa.  Southeast Asian Lichun, Ming, Quan, Roeun, Sokhom, Chau, Hai, Leang, Ly, Nhanh, Phan, Pho, Tha.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 歐, meaning ‘to sing someone's praise’ in ancient Chinese: (i) from the placename Dong Ou (東甌), a fief (in present-day Wenzhou in Zhejiang province) which was granted to Yao in 192  BC . It is said that Yao was a descendant of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (ii) from the placename Ou Yu (歐餘), the name of a fief (located in present-day Zhejiang province) that was granted to Ti, the second son of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 區, originally denoting an ancient vessel for measurement in ancient Chinese: from the personal name of Ou Zhizi (歐治子), an expert sword-maker who lived during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BC ). Some of his descendants inherited Ou (歐) as their surname, simplifying the Chinese character 歐 to 區.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. nameMeanings.name: Cecil
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Transferred use of the surname of a great noble family, which rose to prominence in England during the 16th century. The Cecils were of Welsh origin, and their surname represents an Anglicized form of the Welsh given name Seissylt . In the Middle Ages Cecil was occasionally used as an English form of Latin Caecilius (an old Roman family name derived from the byname Caecus ‘blind’), borne by a minor saint of the 3rd century, a friend of St Cyprian .

nameMeanings.name: ap
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of any of the names beginning with this syllable.

variants.name: Alexander
meanings: From the Latin form of the Greek name Alexandros, from alexein ‘to defend’ + anēr ‘man, warrior’ (genitive andros). The name became extremely popular in the post-classical period, and was borne by several individuals in the New Testament and some early Christian saints. Its use as a common given name throughout Europe, however, derives largely from the fame of Alexander the Great , King of Macedon ( 356–323 bc ), around whom a large body of popular legend grew up in late antiquity, much of which came to be embodied in the medieval ‘Alexander romances’.

variants.name: Alan
meanings: Of Celtic origin and uncertain derivation (possibly a diminutive of a word meaning ‘rock’). It was introduced into England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror , most notably Alan , Earl of Brittany, who was rewarded for his services with vast estates in the newly conquered kingdom. In Britain the variants Allan and Allen are considerably less frequent, and generally represent transferred uses of surname forms, whereas in America all three forms of the name are approximately equally common. See also Alun .

variants.name: Albert
meanings: From an Old French name, Albert, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, displacing the Old English form Æþelbeorht. The name is popular in a variety of forms in Western Europe, and has been traditional in a number of European princely families. It was out of favour in England for centuries, however, and the revival of its popularity in the 19th century was largely in honour of Queen Victoria's consort, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha .

nameMeanings.name: Philip
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From the Greek name Philippos, meaning ‘lover of horses’, from philein ‘to love’ + hippos ‘horse’. This was popular in the classical period and since. It was the name of the father of Alexander the Great . It was also the name of one of Christ's apostles, of a deacon ordained by the apostles after the death of Christ, and of several other early saints. See also Philippa .

historicEvents.title: Wars of the Roses
historicEvents.description: Wars of the Roses was a series of English civil wars for control of the throne of England between the royal families House of Lancaster and the House of York. The name of the war was derived as the red rose was the symbol of the Lancastrians and the white rose was the symbol of the Yorks.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard Cecil ap Philip SEISYLLT of Alt-yr-Ynys was born after 1425, in Monmouthshire, Wales, his father, Sir Richard Cecil, was 8629 and his mother, Margaret Phillips, was 8574. He married Margaret Maud VAUGHAN in 1468, in Burleigh, Gloucestershire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 6 sons and 1 daughter. He died in 1508, in Lincolnshire, England.

Beaufort, Monmouthshire, Pays de Galles, Royaume-Uni
fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard Cecil ap Philip SEISYLLT of Alt-yr-Ynys est né après 1425, à Monmouthshire, Galles, son père, Sir Richard Cecil, avait 8629 ans et sa mère, Margaret Phillips, avait 8574 ans. Il a épousé Margaret Maud VAUGHAN en 1468, à Burleigh, Gloucestershire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 6 fils et 1 fille. Il est décédé en 1508, à Lincolnshire, Angleterre.

Er hat 1468 in Burleigh, Gloucestershire, England, United Kingdom Margaret Maud VAUGHAN geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 6 Söhne und 1 Tochter. Er starb 1508 in Lincolnshire, England.

Lui sposato Margaret Maud VAUGHAN nel 1468, nel Burleigh, Gloucestershire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 6 figli e 1 figlia. Morì nel 1508, nel Lincolnshire, England.

ko.lifeSummary: Richard Cecil ap Philip SEISYLLT of Alt-yr-Ynys(이)가 1425년 이후 웨일스 몬모우스실르에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Richard Cecil(은)는 8629세였고, 그의 어머니 Margaret Phillips(은)는 8574세였다. 그는 1468년 영국 잉글랜드 그루스스터실르 버리에서 Margaret Maud VAUGHAN(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 여섯 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1508년 영국 링코실르에서 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard Cecil ap Philip SEISYLLT of Alt-yr-Ynys родился после 1425 г. в местности монмоутшир, Уэльс, его отцу по имени Sir Richard Cecil было 8629 лет, а матери, которую звали Margaret Phillips, 8574.
